1	"""TSL factory programmer with auto-detect and Google Sheets logging.
2	
3	Autodetects target connection, programs firmware + commissioning timestamp,
4	verifies, power-cycles the target to run briefly, then logs the result to
5	a Google Sheet. Waits for target removal before the next cycle.

44	BOOT_SETTLE_MS = 700

47	LPM_CURRENT_MIN_UA = 1
48	LPM_CURRENT_MAX_UA = 3
49	
50	# WiFi timeouts
51	SNTP_REFRESH_INTERVAL_S = 6000  # refresh SNTP time every 6000 seconds (~100 minutes)

61	LOG         = 6

75	C_PASS    = LT_GREEN

82	        year, month, mday, hour, minute, second, weekday = now[:7]

96	            hour,
97	            weekday,

99	            month,
100	            year % 100,

102	    )

106	    """Program and verify one target. Returns device UUID on success."""

111	        dt = time.ticks_diff(time.ticks_ms(), t0)
112	        print("%02d.%03d %s" % (dt // 1000, dt % 1000, msg))
113

161	
162	# Do wifi connection and logging in a background thread so it doesn't block programming
163	# we share both the pixel display and the log folder with the main thread, so we added semaphore locks
164	# for those (sp and the log folder)
165

167

172

200	                    while flush_once(SHEETS_URL):

204	                    sp.set(LOG, C_FAIL)

207	        else:
208	            if w.is_error():

210	                print("WiFi error detected, status:", w._wlan.status())

212	                sp.set(WIFI, C_PENDING)
213	
214	        time.sleep(5)

218	    print("Testing status pixels...")
219	    sp = StatusPixels(PIXEL_PIN, PIXEL_COUNT)
220	    for color in [RED, GREEN, BLUE,BLACK]:
221	        for i in range(PIXEL_COUNT):
222	            sp.set(i, color)
223	            time.sleep_ms(100)
224	        time.sleep_ms(500)
225

227	    import machine
228	    import network
229	    from target_power import TargetPower, TargetPowerWithDetect
230	    from utils import load_firmware_blocks, firmware_hash
231	
232	    # Start Wifi and get MAC before starting bg thread — both threads accessing WLAN can deadlock
233	    from wifi import WiFi

240	    fw_hash = firmware_hash(firmware_blocks)
241	    print(
242	        "Firmware is %d block(s), %d bytes.\n"
243	        % (len(firmware_blocks), sum(len(data) for _, data in firmware_blocks))
244	    )
245	
246	    # get log ready for both threads
247	    log.init()
248	
249	    # Read calibration file before bg starts file access
250	    try:
251	        r_pullup = TargetPower.load_calibration()
252	        print("Pull-up calibration: %.0f ohm" % r_pullup)
253	        sp.set(CALIBRATE, C_PASS)

275	
276	        print("Target detected.")
277	        sp.set(READY, BLACK)
278	        sp.set(PROGRAM, C_PENDING)
279

281	        status = "fail"
282	        current_ua = None
283	
284	        print("Powering on target...")
285	        power.on()
286	
287	        led.on()
288	        try:
289	            device_uuid = program_once(power, sbw, firmware_blocks)
290	            status = "programmed"
291	            sp.set(PROGRAM, C_PASS)
292	        except Exception as exc:
293	            print("Programming failed: %s" % exc)
294	            sp.set(PROGRAM, C_FAIL)
295	            sp.set(STATE, C_FAIL)
296	        finally:
297	            led.off()
298	
299	        # no need to check current if programming failed
300	        if status == "programmed":
301	            # Reboot target so newly programmed firmware starts running
302	            print("Rebooting target...")
303	            sp.set(BOOT, C_PENDING)
304	            power.off()
305	            time.sleep_ms(REBOOT_OFF_MS)
306	            time.sleep_ms(5000)
307	            power.on()
308	            # Wait for the RV3032 to stabilize after power-up before attempting to communicate with it
309	            print("Waiting %dms for target to start up..." % BOOT_SETTLE_MS)
310	            time.sleep_ms(BOOT_SETTLE_MS)
311	            sp.set(BOOT, C_PASS)
312	
313	            # By the time we get to here, the TSL should be at the "TEST ONLY" screen where power usage is representative. 
314	            # Measure LPM current to test for defects
315	            sp.set(CURRENT, C_PENDING)
316	            print("Switching target power to internal pull-up for current measurement...")
317	            adc = power.power_via_pullup()

319	            current_ua = power.measure_current_ua(adc, r_pullup, count=10, period_ms=10)
320	            time.sleep_ms(1000)
321	            if current_ua is None:
322	                status = "brownout"
323	                print("Brownout — target not in LPM.")
324	                sp.set(CURRENT, C_FAIL)
325	                sp.set(STATE, C_FAIL)
326	            elif current_ua < LPM_CURRENT_MIN_UA or current_ua > LPM_CURRENT_MAX_UA:
327	                status = "lpm_fail"
328	                print("Target current out of LPM spec: %.0f µA" % current_ua)
329	                sp.set(CURRENT, C_FAIL)
330	                sp.set(STATE, C_FAIL)
331	            else:
332	                status = "pass"
333	                print("Target current draw: %.0f µA" % current_ua)
334	                sp.set(CURRENT, C_PASS)
335	                sp.set(STATE, C_PASS)
336	
337	        # Power off target
338	        print("Powering off target and releasing SBW...")
339	        sbw.release()
340	        power.off()
341	
342	        # Queue log row
343	        now = safe_time.gmtime()
344	        timestamp = "%04d-%02d-%02d %02d:%02d:%02d" % (now[0], now[1], now[2], now[3], now[4], now[5])
345	        log_row(SHEETS_URL, [device_uuid or "unknown", timestamp, fw_hash, mac_addr, status, current_ua])
346	
347	        sp.set(LOG,C_PENDING)
348	
349	        print("Waiting for target to be removed...")
350	        power.wait_for_disconnect()
351	        print("Target removed.\n")
352	
353	        #clear progress from previous run
354	        sp.set(PROGRAM, BLACK)
355	        sp.set(BOOT, BLACK)
356	        sp.set(CURRENT, BLACK)
357	        sp.set(LOG, BLACK)
358	        sp.set(STATE, BLACK)
359	
360	for i in range(10):
361	    print(f"Starting in {10 - i} seconds...")
362	    time.sleep(1)
363	    
364	program_loop()

"""TSL factory programmer with auto-detect and Google Sheets logging.

Autodetects target connection, programs firmware + commissioning timestamp,
verifies, power-cycles the target to run briefly, then logs the result to
a Google Sheet. Waits for target removal before the next cycle.

Requires secrets.py with WIFI_SSID, WIFI_PASSWORD, and SHEETS_URL.

Pin connections below.
"""
import time
import _thread
import safe_time
from sbw import SBW
from secrets import WIFI_SSID, WIFI_PASSWORD, WIFI_COUNTRY, SHEETS_URL, FIRMWARE_URL      
from utils import get_device_uuid
import sbw_native
from pixels import *

# Pico Pin | GPIO | Function
# ---------|------|----------
#       29 | GP22 | SBWTDIO
#       31 | GP26 | SBWTCK (must have ADC)
#       32 | GP27 | VCC
SBW_PIN_CLOCK = 26
SBW_PIN_DATA = 22
SBW_PIN_POWER = 27
POWER_SETTLE_MS = 20
PIXEL_PIN = 28

FIRMWARE_FILE_NAME = "tsl-calibre-msp.txt"

# from part datasheets
DEVICE_DESCRIPTOR_ADDRESS = 0x1A00
DEVICE_DESCRIPTOR_LENGTH = 0x12

# beging of USER FRAM
TIMESTAMP_ADDRESS = 0x1800

# based on part and circuit and firmware
REBOOT_OFF_MS  = 100
# This is dominated by the 0.5s the TSL must wait for the RV3032 to stabilize after power-up.
# This param is tSTART and speced 0.1s typical, 0.5s max. 
BOOT_SETTLE_MS = 700

# emperical acceptable current range for a TSL
LPM_CURRENT_MIN_UA = 1
LPM_CURRENT_MAX_UA = 3

# WiFi timeouts
SNTP_REFRESH_INTERVAL_S = 6000  # refresh SNTP time every 6000 seconds (~100 minutes)

# Pixel indices for headless status strip.
READY   = 0
STATE   = 1

# progress
PROGRAM     = 3
BOOT        = 4
CURRENT     = 5
LOG         = 6

# Startup status steps
CALIBRATE   = 7
WIFI        = 8
NTP         = 9
UPDATE      = 10
SPACER      = 11
# ===============
PIXEL_COUNT = 12

# Semantic pixel colors
C_READY   = GREEN
C_PENDING = LT_YELLOW
C_PASS    = LT_GREEN
C_FAIL    = RED
C_ACTIVE  = YELLOW      # currently active / in-progress


def build_timestamp_bytes(now):
    if isinstance(now, tuple):
        year, month, mday, hour, minute, second, weekday = now[:7]
    else:
        second = now.tm_sec
        minute = now.tm_min
        hour = now.tm_hour
        weekday = now.tm_wday
        mday = now.tm_mday
        month = now.tm_mon
        year = now.tm_year

    return bytes(
        [
            second,
            minute,
            hour,
            weekday,
            mday,
            month,
            year % 100,
        ]
    )


def program_once(power, sbw, firmware_blocks):
    """Program and verify one target. Returns device UUID on success."""
    t0 = time.ticks_ms()

    def tp(msg):
        """Print with ##.### seconds timestamp."""
        dt = time.ticks_diff(time.ticks_ms(), t0)
        print("%02d.%03d %s" % (dt // 1000, dt % 1000, msg))

    now = safe_time.gmtime()

    tp("Reading JTAG ID...")
    ok, jtag_id = sbw.read_id()
    if not ok or jtag_id != sbw_native.JTAG_ID_EXPECTED:
        raise RuntimeError("expected JTAG ID 0x%02X, found 0x%02X" % (sbw_native.JTAG_ID_EXPECTED, jtag_id))

    tp("Reading device descriptor...")
    ok, dd_bytes = sbw.read_bytes(DEVICE_DESCRIPTOR_ADDRESS, DEVICE_DESCRIPTOR_LENGTH)
    if not ok or len(dd_bytes) != DEVICE_DESCRIPTOR_LENGTH:
        raise RuntimeError("failed to read device descriptor")

    device_uuid = get_device_uuid(dd_bytes)
    tp("Device UUID is %s" % device_uuid)

    # Write timestamp + firmware
    timestamp_data = build_timestamp_bytes(now)
    tp("Writing commissioning timestamp...")
    if not sbw.write_bytes(TIMESTAMP_ADDRESS, timestamp_data):
        raise RuntimeError("write failed for timestamp at 0x%05X" % TIMESTAMP_ADDRESS)

    for index, (address, data) in enumerate(firmware_blocks, start=1):
        tp("Writing firmware block %d/%d addr=0x%05X len=%d..." % (
            index, len(firmware_blocks), address & 0xFFFFF, len(data)))
        if not sbw.write_bytes(address, data):
            raise RuntimeError("write failed at 0x%05X" % (address & 0xFFFFF))

    # Verify all
    tp("Verifying commissioning timestamp...")
    ok, _ = sbw.verify_bytes(TIMESTAMP_ADDRESS, timestamp_data)
    if not ok:
        raise RuntimeError("verify failed for timestamp at 0x%05X" % TIMESTAMP_ADDRESS)

    for index, (address, data) in enumerate(firmware_blocks, start=1):
        tp("Verifying firmware block %d/%d addr=0x%05X len=%d..." % (
            index, len(firmware_blocks), address & 0xFFFFF, len(data)))
        ok, _ = sbw.verify_bytes(address, data)
        if not ok:
            raise RuntimeError("verify failed at 0x%05X" % (address & 0xFFFFF))

    tp("All blocks verified.")

    return device_uuid


import log
from log import log_row, flush_once, pending_count

# Do wifi connection and logging in a background thread so it doesn't block programming
# we share both the pixel display and the log folder with the main thread, so we added semaphore locks
# for those (sp and the log folder)

def bg_thread(sp,w):

    print("Background thread started.")

    import ntptime
    import safe_time

    # did we start up with a backlog?
    if pending_count() > 0:
        print("Backlog detected: %d pending rows" % pending_count())
        sp.set(LOG, C_PENDING)
    else:
        sp.set(LOG, C_PASS)

    next_sntp_refresh = time.time() 

    while True:

        if w.connect():
            sp.set(WIFI, C_PASS)

            if (time.time() >= next_sntp_refresh):
                sp.set(NTP, C_PENDING)
                try:
                    safe_time.settime(ntptime)
                    sp.set(NTP, C_PASS)
                except Exception:
                    sp.set(NTP, C_FAIL)
                next_sntp_refresh = time.time() + SNTP_REFRESH_INTERVAL_S       


            if (pending_count() > 0):
                sp.set(LOG, C_PENDING)
                try:
                    while flush_once(SHEETS_URL):
                        pass
                    sp.set(LOG, C_PASS)
                except Exception as e:
                    sp.set(LOG, C_FAIL)
                    print("Failed to flush log: %s" % e)

        else:
            if w.is_error():
                sp.set(WIFI, C_FAIL)
                print("WiFi error detected, status:", w._wlan.status())
            else:
                sp.set(WIFI, C_PENDING)

        time.sleep(5)

def program_loop():

    print("Testing status pixels...")
    sp = StatusPixels(PIXEL_PIN, PIXEL_COUNT)
    for color in [RED, GREEN, BLUE,BLACK]:
        for i in range(PIXEL_COUNT):
            sp.set(i, color)
            time.sleep_ms(100)
        time.sleep_ms(500)

    print("Initializing modules...")
    import machine
    import network
    from target_power import TargetPower, TargetPowerWithDetect
    from utils import load_firmware_blocks, firmware_hash

    # Start Wifi and get MAC before starting bg thread — both threads accessing WLAN can deadlock
    from wifi import WiFi
    w = WiFi(WIFI_SSID, WIFI_PASSWORD, WIFI_COUNTRY)
    mac_addr = w.mac_hex()

    # load firmware touches the file system, so load it before starting the background thread
    print("Loading firmware file %s into memory..." % FIRMWARE_FILE_NAME)
    firmware_blocks = load_firmware_blocks(FIRMWARE_FILE_NAME)
    fw_hash = firmware_hash(firmware_blocks)
    print(
        "Firmware is %d block(s), %d bytes.\n"
        % (len(firmware_blocks), sum(len(data) for _, data in firmware_blocks))
    )

    # get log ready for both threads
    log.init()

    # Read calibration file before bg starts file access
    try:
        r_pullup = TargetPower.load_calibration()
        print("Pull-up calibration: %.0f ohm" % r_pullup)
        sp.set(CALIBRATE, C_PASS)
    except Exception as e:
        print("Failed to load pull-up calibration: %s" % e)
        sp.set(CALIBRATE, C_FAIL)
        sp.set(READY, C_FAIL)   
        print("HALTED. Run current_measure_calibrate to fix.")
        while True:
            time.sleep_ms(1000)


    print("Starting background thread...")
    _thread.start_new_thread(bg_thread, (sp,w))

    power = TargetPowerWithDetect(SBW_PIN_POWER, SBW_PIN_CLOCK, POWER_SETTLE_MS)
    sbw = SBW(SBW_PIN_CLOCK, SBW_PIN_DATA)
    led = machine.Pin("LED", machine.Pin.OUT, value=0)


    while True:
        print("Waiting for target...")
        sp.set(READY, C_READY)
        power.wait_for_connect()

        print("Target detected.")
        sp.set(READY, BLACK)
        sp.set(PROGRAM, C_PENDING)

        device_uuid = None
        status = "fail"
        current_ua = None

        print("Powering on target...")
        power.on()

        led.on()
        try:
            device_uuid = program_once(power, sbw, firmware_blocks)
            status = "programmed"
            sp.set(PROGRAM, C_PASS)
        except Exception as exc:
            print("Programming failed: %s" % exc)
            sp.set(PROGRAM, C_FAIL)
            sp.set(STATE, C_FAIL)
        finally:
            led.off()

        # no need to check current if programming failed
        if status == "programmed":
            # Reboot target so newly programmed firmware starts running
            print("Rebooting target...")
            sp.set(BOOT, C_PENDING)
            power.off()
            time.sleep_ms(REBOOT_OFF_MS)
            time.sleep_ms(5000)
            power.on()
            # Wait for the RV3032 to stabilize after power-up before attempting to communicate with it
            print("Waiting %dms for target to start up..." % BOOT_SETTLE_MS)
            time.sleep_ms(BOOT_SETTLE_MS)
            sp.set(BOOT, C_PASS)

            # By the time we get to here, the TSL should be at the "TEST ONLY" screen where power usage is representative. 
            # Measure LPM current to test for defects
            sp.set(CURRENT, C_PENDING)
            print("Switching target power to internal pull-up for current measurement...")
            adc = power.power_via_pullup()
            time.sleep_ms(1000)
            current_ua = power.measure_current_ua(adc, r_pullup, count=10, period_ms=10)
            time.sleep_ms(1000)
            if current_ua is None:
                status = "brownout"
                print("Brownout — target not in LPM.")
                sp.set(CURRENT, C_FAIL)
                sp.set(STATE, C_FAIL)
            elif current_ua < LPM_CURRENT_MIN_UA or current_ua > LPM_CURRENT_MAX_UA:
                status = "lpm_fail"
                print("Target current out of LPM spec: %.0f µA" % current_ua)
                sp.set(CURRENT, C_FAIL)
                sp.set(STATE, C_FAIL)
            else:
                status = "pass"
                print("Target current draw: %.0f µA" % current_ua)
                sp.set(CURRENT, C_PASS)
                sp.set(STATE, C_PASS)

        # Power off target
        print("Powering off target and releasing SBW...")
        sbw.release()
        power.off()

        # Queue log row
        now = safe_time.gmtime()
        timestamp = "%04d-%02d-%02d %02d:%02d:%02d" % (now[0], now[1], now[2], now[3], now[4], now[5])
        log_row(SHEETS_URL, [device_uuid or "unknown", timestamp, fw_hash, mac_addr, status, current_ua])

        sp.set(LOG,C_PENDING)

        print("Waiting for target to be removed...")
        power.wait_for_disconnect()
        print("Target removed.\n")

        #clear progress from previous run
        sp.set(PROGRAM, BLACK)
        sp.set(BOOT, BLACK)
        sp.set(CURRENT, BLACK)
        sp.set(LOG, BLACK)
        sp.set(STATE, BLACK)

for i in range(10):
    print(f"Starting in {10 - i} seconds...")
    time.sleep(1)
    
program_loop()

"""WiFi connection management for Pico W."""
import network
import time

# Don't mess with this stuff. This sequencing seems to work most of the time, others fail - even
# ones copied diretcly from the Pico W examples sometimes fail to connect reliably.
# And this stuff is basically non-documented. :(

# Do note that sometime the Wifi just will not connect on the first try ever. This code
# does seem to always connect on the 2nd try with my Wifi. 


class WiFi:
    def __init__(self, ssid, password, country=None, retry_interval=30):
        self._ssid = ssid
        self._password = password

        if country:
            print("Setting WiFi country to", country)
            network.country(country)

        # station rather than AP
        self._wlan = network.WLAN(network.STA_IF)

        #load network stack
        self._wlan.active(True)

        # By default the wireless chip will active power-saving mode when it is idle, which might lead it to being less responsive. If
        # you are running a server or need more responsiveness, you can change this by toggling the power mode.
        self._wlan.config(pm = 0xa11140) 

        self._next_retry = time.time()
        self.retry_interval = retry_interval

    def is_error(self):
        """Return True if the WiFi is in a stuck error state (LINK_FAIL or BADAUTH)."""
        return self._wlan.status() in (-1, -3)

    def connect(self):
        """Connect to WiFi, returns True on success.

        The CYW43 radio firmware has an intermittent cold-start bug where
        the first association/authentication attempt fails ~50% of the time
        (EV_SET_SSID returns failure status, or WPA2 handshake fails).
        The delay between active() and connect() is irrelevant — the chip
        is ready, it just sometimes fails the handshake.

        Once in LINK_FAIL (-1) or BADAUTH (-3), the driver is stuck —
        it won't self-recover. We detect this, disconnect (which resets
        wifi_join_state via EV_DISASSOC), and retry within the timeout.
        Second attempt almost always succeeds.
        """

        if (self._wlan.status() <= 0 and time.time() >= self._next_retry):
            print("Failed to connect, status:", self._wlan.status())
            print("Disconnecting due to failed connection attempt...")
            # we seem to need this sometimes to reset the stuck state
            self._wlan.disconnect()
            time.sleep(1)
            print("Re-attempting connection...")
            self._wlan.connect(self._ssid, self._password)
            time.sleep(1)
            self._next_retry = time.time() +  self.retry_interval

        return self._wlan.isconnected()
        

    def is_connected(self):
        """Return True if WiFi is currently connected."""
        return self._wlan.isconnected()

    def mac_hex(self):
        """Return the MAC address as an uppercase hex string."""
        return "".join("%02X" % b for b in self._wlan.config("mac"))

    def ip(self):
        """Return the current IP address string, or None if not connected."""
        if self._wlan.isconnected():
            return self._wlan.ifconfig()[0]
        return None

file.content:
"""Append rows to a Google Sheet via Apps Script, with offline resilience.

Foreground: writes each request as a file in pending/, never blocks.
Background: drains pending/ in FIFO order, deleting files on success.

The pending/ directory is the queue. Files are written as .tmp_* (invisible
to background), then atomically renamed to req_* (visible). This guarantees
the background never sees a partially written file. Both os.rename() and
os.remove() are atomic on the Pico's littlefs2 filesystem.
"""
import json
import os
import socket
import ssl
import time
import _thread

PENDING_DIR = "pending"

_seq = 0
_lock = None


def init():
    """Initialize the log module. Call once at startup.

    Creates the pending directory, scans for existing files to avoid
    overwriting on reboot, and allocates a lock for thread safety.
    """
    global _seq, _lock

    try:
        os.mkdir(PENDING_DIR)
    except OSError:
        pass

    try:
        names = [n for n in os.listdir(PENDING_DIR) if n.startswith("req_")]
        if names:
            _seq = max(int(n[4:]) for n in names)
    except OSError:
        pass

    _lock = _thread.allocate_lock()


def log_row(url, data):
    """Queue a row for sending. Returns immediately, never blocks."""
    global _seq
    _seq += 1
    json_str = json.dumps(data)
    tmp_path = "%s/.tmp_%06d" % (PENDING_DIR, _seq)
    req_path = "%s/req_%06d" % (PENDING_DIR, _seq)
    _lock.acquire()
    try:
        with open(tmp_path, "w") as f:
            f.write(json_str)
        os.rename(tmp_path, req_path)
    finally:
        _lock.release()


def pending_count():
    """Return the number of unsent requests in the queue."""
    _lock.acquire()
    try:
        return sum(1 for name in os.listdir(PENDING_DIR) if name.startswith("req_"))
    except OSError:
        return 0
    finally:
        _lock.release()


def _get_json(url):
    """GET a URL over HTTPS, return parsed JSON response."""
    proto, _, host_path = url.split("/", 2)
    host_port, path = host_path.split("/", 1)
    path = "/" + path
    port = 443 if proto == "https:" else 80
    if ":" in host_port:
        host, port = host_port.rsplit(":", 1)
        port = int(port)
    else:
        host = host_port

    hdr = "GET %s HTTP/1.0\r\nHost: %s\r\n\r\n" % (path, host)
    s = socket.socket()
    s.connect(socket.getaddrinfo(host, port)[0][-1])
    if proto == "https:":
        s = ssl.wrap_socket(s, server_hostname=host)
    s.write(hdr.encode())

    # Skip status line and headers
    while s.readline().strip():
        pass
    resp_body = s.read()
    s.close()
    return json.loads(resp_body)


def flush_once(url):
    """Try to send one pending request. Returns True if one was sent."""
    _lock.acquire()
    try:
        names = sorted(n for n in os.listdir(PENDING_DIR) if n.startswith("req_"))
        if not names:
            return False
        name = names[0]
        path = "%s/%s" % (PENDING_DIR, name)
        with open(path, "r") as f:
            data = json.loads(f.read())
    finally:
        _lock.release()

    # Network I/O outside the lock
    result = _post_json(url, data)
    if result.get("message") == "Data appended successfully":
        _lock.acquire()
        try:
            os.remove(path)
        finally:
            _lock.release()
        return True
    else:
        print("Sheet rejected row: %s" % result.get("message", "unknown"))
        raise RuntimeError("Sheet rejected row: %s" % result.get("message", "unknown"))


def _post_json(url, data, max_redirects=3):
    """POST JSON to a URL, following redirects. Returns parsed response JSON.

    MicroPython's requests library converts POST to GET on redirect,
    which breaks Google Apps Script. This uses raw sockets instead.
    """
    body = json.dumps(data).encode()

    for _ in range(max_redirects + 1):
        proto, _, host_path = url.split("/", 2)
        host_port, path = host_path.split("/", 1)
        path = "/" + path
        use_ssl = proto == "https:"
        port = 443 if use_ssl else 80
        if ":" in host_port:
            host, port = host_port.rsplit(":", 1)
            port = int(port)
        else:
            host = host_port

        hdr = "POST %s HTTP/1.0\r\nHost: %s\r\nContent-Type: application/json\r\nContent-Length: %d\r\n\r\n" % (path, host, len(body))

        s = socket.socket()
        s.connect(socket.getaddrinfo(host, port)[0][-1])
        if use_ssl:
            s = ssl.wrap_socket(s, server_hostname=host)
        s.write(hdr.encode() + body)

        # Read status line
        line = s.readline().decode()
        status = int(line.split(" ", 2)[1])

        # Read headers
        location = None
        while True:
            hline = s.readline().decode().strip()
            if not hline:
                break
            if hline.lower().startswith("location:"):
                location = hline.split(":", 1)[1].strip()

        if status in (301, 302, 307, 308) and location:
            # Google Apps Script redirects POST to a GET response URL
            resp_body = s.read()
            s.close()
            return _get_json(location)

        # Read body
        resp_body = s.read()
        s.close()
        return json.loads(resp_body)

    raise RuntimeError("too many redirects")

content.text:
Perfect! I've thoroughly read the complete codebase. Here's my comprehensive analysis:

## 1. List of All Files and Their Roles

| File | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| **main.py** | Single Python file containing the entire application - a WiFi connectivity test with background task management and LED status indication |

## 2. How the Background Task is Started and Managed

The background task is started on **line 98**:
```python
_thread.start_new_thread(network_thread, ())
```

- Uses MicroPython's `_thread` module to spawn a new thread
- No cleanup mechanism exists; the thread runs indefinitely once started
- The function `network_thread()` (lines 56-77) is the background task entry point
- No explicit thread termination or management after startup

## 3. How State/Flags are Shared Between Main and Background

**Global shared state variable** (line 13):
```python
state = 0
```

State values and their meanings:
- **0** = WiFi down (not connected, no IP)
- **1** = Connecting (WiFi connect in progress)
- **2** = Probe failed (WiFi has IP but no real network connectivity)
- **3** = Connected (WiFi connected and network probe succeeded)

**Write pattern (background thread only):**
- Line 23: `state = 1` (in `connect_wifi()`)
- Line 62: `state = 0` (in `network_thread()`)
- Line 71: `state = 2` (in `network_thread()`)
- Line 76: `state = 3` (in `network_thread()`)

**Read pattern (main thread only):**
- Line 101: `s = state` (snapshot read)

This is a unidirectional communication pattern: **background writes, foreground reads**. No locks or synchronization primitives are used, relying on the fact that reads/writes of small integers are atomic in Python.

## 4. The Exact Sequencing of Operations (WiFi Connect, Task Creation, Error Handling)

**Startup sequence (lines 97-99):**
1. Print "Starting connectivity test"
2. Start background thread with `network_thread`
3. Enter foreground main loop

**Background thread loop (lines 59-77):**

```
Iteration starts:
├─ Check if WiFi has IP (wlan.status() != STAT_GOT_IP)
│  ├─ YES: Go to connectivity probe
│  └─ NO: Call connect_wifi()
│     ├─ Set wlan active(True)
│     ├─ Disconnect any existing connection
│     ├─ Set state = 1 (connecting)
│     ├─ Call wlan.connect(SSID, PASSWORD)
│     ├─ Poll status up to 20 times (1 second each)
│     │  ├─ If STAT_GOT_IP: return True
│     │  └─ If status < 0: return False
│     ├─ If timeout: return False
│     └─ If connect failed: set state = 0, sleep 1s, continue
│
├─ Connectivity probe (check_connectivity())
│  ├─ Create TCP socket to 8.8.8.8:53 with 5s timeout
│  ├─ SUCCESS: continue to success path
│  └─ OSError: Disconnect WiFi, set state = 2, sleep 1s, continue
│
└─ Success path:
   └─ Set state = 3 (connected), sleep 1s
```

**Foreground main loop (lines 100-112):**
1. Snapshot current state value
2. Based on state, execute corresponding LED blink pattern:
   - state=0: 1 blink (down)
   - state=1: 2 blinks (connecting)
   - state=2: 3 blinks (probe failed)
   - state=3: continuous toggle 500ms on/off (connected)

## 5. Synchronization Mechanisms Used

**No explicit synchronization:**
- **No mutexes, locks, or condition variables** are used
- **Race condition exists** on line 101 where foreground reads `state` while background writes to it
- **Mitigation**: Relies on MicroPython/Python GIL (Global Interpreter Lock) and atomic integer reads/writes
- **Assumption**: Integer assignment is atomic enough for this use case

The design trades safety for simplicity—acceptable for a test application on single-core Raspberry Pi Pico.

## 6. How Errors/Failures in the Background Task Are Handled

**WiFi connection failures:**
- Lines 61-64: If `connect_wifi()` returns False, set `state = 0` and retry after 1-second sleep
- No exception handling; connection failures are detected via status polling

**Network connectivity failures:**
- Lines 68-73: If TCP probe to 8.8.8.8:53 raises OSError (connection timeout/refused):
  - Print error message
  - Disconnect WiFi
  - Set `state = 2` (probe_failed)
  - Sleep 1 second and retry the entire loop

**Infinite retry loop:**
- Lines 59: `while True:` ensures the background thread never exits
- Continuously cycles: WiFi connect → connectivity probe → state update → sleep → repeat
- No maximum retry limits or exponential backoff
- CHECK_INTERVAL (1 second) is the only delay between retry cycles

**No error propagation:**
- Errors in the background thread are only printed to console
- The foreground thread has no way to know about specific error details
- All error information is encoded in the 4-state system

## 1. List of All Files and Their Roles

| File | Purpose |
|------|---------|

## 2. How the Background Task is Started and Managed

The background task is started on **line 98**:

- No explicit thread termination or management after startup

## 3. How State/Flags are Shared Between Main and Background

**Global shared state variable** (line 13):
```python
state = 0
```

State values and their meanings:
- **0** = WiFi down (not connected, no IP)
- **1** = Connecting (WiFi connect in progress)
- **2** = Probe failed (WiFi has IP but no real network connectivity)
- **3** = Connected (WiFi connected and network probe succeeded)

**Write pattern (background thread only):**
- Line 23: `state = 1` (in `connect_wifi()`)
- Line 62: `state = 0` (in `network_thread()`)
- Line 71: `state = 2` (in `network_thread()`)
- Line 76: `state = 3` (in `network_thread()`)

**Read pattern (main thread only):**
- Line 101: `s = state` (snapshot read)

This is a unidirectional communication pattern: **background writes, foreground reads**. No locks or synchronization primitives are used, relying on the fact that reads/writes of small integers are atomic in Python.

## 4. The Exact Sequencing of Operations (WiFi Connect, Task Creation, Error Handling)

**Startup sequence (lines 97-99):**
1. Print "Starting connectivity test"
2. Start background thread with `network_thread`
3. Enter foreground main loop

**Background thread loop (lines 59-77):**

```
Iteration starts:

│  ├─ YES: Go to connectivity probe
│  └─ NO: Call connect_wifi()
│     ├─ Set wlan active(True)
│     ├─ Disconnect any existing connection
│     ├─ Set state = 1 (connecting)
│     ├─ Call wlan.connect(SSID, PASSWORD)
│     ├─ Poll status up to 20 times (1 second each)
│     │  ├─ If STAT_GOT_IP: return True
│     │  └─ If status < 0: return False
│     ├─ If timeout: return False
│     └─ If connect failed: set state = 0, sleep 1s, continue
│
├─ Connectivity probe (check_connectivity())
│  ├─ Create TCP socket to 8.8.8.8:53 with 5s timeout
│  ├─ SUCCESS: continue to success path
│  └─ OSError: Disconnect WiFi, set state = 2, sleep 1s, continue
│
└─ Success path:
   └─ Set state = 3 (connected), sleep 1s
```

**Foreground main loop (lines 100-112):**
1. Snapshot current state value
2. Based on state, execute corresponding LED blink pattern:
   - state=0: 1 blink (down)
   - state=1: 2 blinks (connecting)
   - state=2: 3 blinks (probe failed)
   - state=3: continuous toggle 500ms on/off (connected)

**No explicit synchronization:**
- **No mutexes, locks, or condition variables** are used
- **Race condition exists** on line 101 where foreground reads `state` while background writes to it
- **Mitigation**: Relies on MicroPython/Python GIL (Global Interpreter Lock) and atomic integer reads/writes
- **Assumption**: Integer assignment is atomic enough for this use case

The design trades safety for simplicity—acceptable for a test application on single-core Raspberry Pi Pico.

## 6. How Errors/Failures in the Background Task Are Handled

**WiFi connection failures:**
- Lines 61-64: If `connect_wifi()` returns False, set `state = 0` and retry after 1-second sleep
- No exception handling; connection failures are detected via status polling

**Network connectivity failures:**
- Lines 68-73: If TCP probe to 8.8.8.8:53 raises OSError (connection timeout/refused):
  - Print error message
  - Disconnect WiFi
  - Set `state = 2` (probe_failed)
  - Sleep 1 second and retry the entire loop

**Infinite retry loop:**
- Lines 59: `while True:` ensures the background thread never exits
- Continuously cycles: WiFi connect → connectivity probe → state update → sleep → repeat
- No maximum retry limits or exponential backoff
- CHECK_INTERVAL (1 second) is the only delay between retry cycles

**No error propagation:**
- Errors in the background thread are only printed to console
- The foreground thread has no way to know about specific error details
- All error information is encoded in the 4-state system

file.content:
import network
import socket
import time
import _thread
from machine import Pin

SSID = "josh.com"
PASSWORD = "nancy123"
CHECK_INTERVAL = 1

# Shared state: only written by background thread, read by foreground
# 0=down, 1=connecting, 2=probe_failed, 3=connected
state = 0

wlan = network.WLAN(network.STA_IF)


def connect_wifi():
    """Disconnect, reconnect to WiFi, and wait for IP. Returns True if connected."""
    global state
    wlan.active(True)
    wlan.disconnect()
    state = 1
    print("Connecting to WiFi:", SSID)
    wlan.connect(SSID, PASSWORD)

    last_status = None
    for i in range(20):
        status = wlan.status()
        if status != last_status:
            print("  WiFi status:", status)
            last_status = status
        if status == network.STAT_GOT_IP:
            print("WiFi connected:", wlan.ipconfig("addr4"))
            return True
        if status < 0:
            print("WiFi failed, status:", status)
            return False
        time.sleep(1)

    print("WiFi connect timed out after 20s, status:", wlan.status())
    return False


def check_connectivity():
    """TCP connect to 8.8.8.8:53 to verify real network connectivity.
    Raises OSError if no connectivity (expected)."""
    s = socket.socket()
    s.settimeout(5)
    try:
        s.connect(("8.8.8.8", 53))
    finally:
        s.close()


def network_thread():
    """Background thread: manage WiFi and probe connectivity."""
    global state
    while True:
        if wlan.status() != network.STAT_GOT_IP:
            if not connect_wifi():
                state = 0
                time.sleep(CHECK_INTERVAL)
                continue

        try:
            check_connectivity()
        except OSError as e:
            print("No connectivity:", e)
            wlan.disconnect()
            state = 2
            time.sleep(CHECK_INTERVAL)
            continue

        print("Connected")
        state = 3
        time.sleep(CHECK_INTERVAL)


# --- Foreground: LED display ---

led = Pin("LED", Pin.OUT)
led.off()


def blink(n):
    """Blink LED n times (100ms on, 100ms gap each), then 500ms off."""
    for i in range(n):
        led.on()
        time.sleep_ms(100)
        led.off()
        if i < n - 1:
            time.sleep_ms(100)
    time.sleep_ms(500)


print("Starting connectivity test")
_thread.start_new_thread(network_thread, ())

while True:
    s = state
    if s == 0:
        blink(1)
    elif s == 1:
        blink(2)
    elif s == 2:
        blink(3)
    elif s == 3:
        led.on()
        time.sleep_ms(500)
        led.off()
        time.sleep_ms(500)

"""Thread-safe time access for shared RTC between foreground and background."""
import time
import _thread

_lock = _thread.allocate_lock()


def gmtime():
    """Thread-safe wrapper for time.gmtime()."""
    _lock.acquire()
    try:
        return time.gmtime()
    finally:
        _lock.release()


def settime(ntptime):
    """Thread-safe wrapper for ntptime.settime()."""
    _lock.acquire()
    try:
        ntptime.settime()
    finally:
        _lock.release()

file.content:
"""WS2812B pixel driver using PIO.

Drives a WS2812B LED strip directly via a PIO state machine. No
dependency on MicroPython's built-in neopixel module.

Usage:
    from pixels import *
    px = Pixels(28)                          # GP28, state machine 0
    px.show([RED, GREEN, BLUE, BLACK, BLACK]) # 5 pixels
    px.show([rgb(255, 128, 0)] * 5)          # all orange

    sp = StatusPixels(28, 5)                 # 5-pixel status strip
    sp.set(0, GREEN)                         # set pixel 0 to green
    sp.set(2, RED)                           # set pixel 2 to red
    sp.clear()                               # all off
"""
import time
import rp2
import machine

import _thread

@rp2.asm_pio(sideset_init=rp2.PIO.OUT_LOW, out_shiftdir=rp2.PIO.SHIFT_LEFT,
             autopull=True, pull_thresh=24)
def _ws2812():
    wrap_target()
    label("bitloop")
    out(x, 1)               .side(0) [2]
    jmp(not_x, "do_zero")   .side(1) [1]
    jmp("bitloop")           .side(1) [4]
    label("do_zero")
    nop()                    .side(0) [4]
    wrap()


def rgb(r, g, b):
    """Pack 8-bit RGB into a 32-bit GRB word for WS2812B."""
    return (g << 16) | (r << 8) | b


# 16 common saturated colors
BLACK   = rgb(0, 0, 0)
RED     = rgb(255, 0, 0)
GREEN   = rgb(0, 255, 0)
BLUE    = rgb(0, 0, 255)
CYAN    = rgb(0, 255, 255)
MAGENTA = rgb(255, 0, 255)
YELLOW  = rgb(255, 255, 0)
WHITE   = rgb(255, 255, 255)
ORANGE  = rgb(255, 128, 0)
PURPLE  = rgb(128, 0, 255)
PINK    = rgb(255, 0, 128)
LIME    = rgb(128, 255, 0)
AQUA    = rgb(0, 128, 255)
NAVY    = rgb(0, 0, 128)
MAROON  = rgb(128, 0, 0)
OLIVE   = rgb(128, 128, 0)

LT_WHITE  = rgb(64, 64, 64)
LT_RED    = rgb(64, 0, 0)
LT_GREEN  = rgb(0, 64, 0)
LT_BLUE   = rgb(0, 0, 64)
LT_YELLOW = rgb(40, 16, 0)


class Pixels:
    def __init__(self, pin, sm_id=0):
        """Initialize PIO state machine for WS2812B on the given GPIO pin.

        pin: GPIO number for the data line
        sm_id: PIO state machine index (0-11 on RP2350)
        """
        self._sm = rp2.StateMachine(sm_id, _ws2812,
            freq=8_000_000, sideset_base=machine.Pin(pin))
        self._sm.active(1)

        # make sure we can call from both threads without garbling display
        self.lock = _thread.allocate_lock()

    def show(self, words):
        """Write pixel data to the strip.

        words: list/tuple of 32-bit GRB values (one per pixel), as
        returned by rgb() or the color constants. Top 8 bits are ignored.
        """
        self.lock.acquire()
        try:
            for w in words:
                self._sm.put(w, 8)
            time.sleep_ms(1)  # wait for PIO to drain + 50µs reset gap to latch
        finally:
            self.lock.release()


class StatusPixels:
    """Buffered pixel strip that remembers state between updates.

    Each call to set() updates one pixel and refreshes the whole strip.
    """

    def __init__(self, pin, n, sm_id=0):
        self._px = Pixels(pin, sm_id)
        self._buf = [BLACK] * n

    def set(self, index, color):
        """Set one pixel and refresh the strip."""
        self._buf[index] = color
        self._px.show(self._buf)

    def clear(self):
        """Turn all pixels off."""
        self._buf = [BLACK] * len(self._buf)
        self._px.show(self._buf)

input.content:
# Plan: Apply simple-network-test pattern to program_tsl.py bg_thread

## Context

The background thread in `program_tsl.py` has been unreliable. A standalone test bench in `simple-network-test/main.py` uses a simpler, linear pattern that works reliably. Goal: restructure `bg_thread` to match the test bench's shape, substituting the TCP probe with the actual HTTPS POST (log flush).

## Key structural difference: test bench vs production

**Test bench (working):**
```
while True:
    if no IP → reconnect; if fail → state=down; sleep; continue
    try probe → except → disconnect; state=error; sleep; continue
    state=connected; sleep
```

**Production (problematic):**
```
while True:
    if w.connect() → NTP; flush all logs
    else → check error state
    sleep 5
```

The test bench's strength is its **linear flow with early-exit `continue`** — each step either succeeds (fall through) or fails (disconnect, set error state, `continue` back to top).

## Complications identified

### 1. `WiFi.connect()` doesn't match the test bench pattern
The current `WiFi.connect()` gates reconnection behind a 30s `retry_interval` and returns `isconnected()` without polling for success. The test bench does a full disconnect → connect → poll-for-20s cycle every time.

**Fix:** Add a `reconnect()` method to `WiFi` that mirrors the test bench's `connect_wifi()`: disconnect, connect, poll status for up to 20s. Keep existing `connect()` and `is_connected()` for other callers if needed.

### 2. Socket leaks in `log.py` on failure
`_post_json()` and `_get_json()` don't wrap socket operations in try/finally. If a flush fails mid-request (DNS timeout, SSL error, etc.), the socket leaks. With the retry pattern, leaked sockets accumulate and can prevent future connections.

**Fix:** Add try/finally to `_post_json()` and `_get_json()` to ensure `s.close()` on any exception.

### 3. NTP failure should be non-fatal
In the test bench there's only one operation (probe). In production we have NTP + flush. NTP failure shouldn't trigger a WiFi disconnect — it's a separate UDP service. Only flush failure (our "probe equivalent") should trigger the disconnect/retry cycle.

## Changes

### File 1: `wifi.py` — Add `reconnect()` method

Add a method that mirrors the test bench's `connect_wifi()`:

```python
def reconnect(self):
    """Full disconnect/reconnect cycle with polling. Returns True if connected."""
    self._wlan.disconnect()
```

# Plan: Apply simple-network-test pattern to program_tsl.py bg_thread

## Context

The background thread in `program_tsl.py` has been unreliable. A standalone test bench in `simple-network-test/main.py` uses a simpler, linear pattern that works reliably. Goal: restructure `bg_thread` to match the test bench's shape, substituting the TCP probe with the actual HTTPS POST (log flush).

## Key structural difference: test bench vs production

**Test bench (working):**
```
while True:
    if no IP → reconnect; if fail → state=down; sleep; continue
    try probe → except → disconnect; state=error; sleep; continue
    state=connected; sleep
```

**Production (problematic):**
```
while True:
    if w.connect() → NTP; flush all logs
    else → check error state
    sleep 5
```

The test bench's strength is its **linear flow with early-exit `continue`** — each step either succeeds (fall through) or fails (disconnect, set error state, `continue` back to top).

## Complications identified

### 1. `WiFi.connect()` doesn't match the test bench pattern
The current `WiFi.connect()` gates reconnection behind a 30s `retry_interval` and returns `isconnected()` without polling for success. The test bench does a full disconnect → connect → poll-for-20s cycle every time.

**Fix:** Add a `reconnect()` method to `WiFi` that mirrors the test bench's `connect_wifi()`: disconnect, connect, poll status for up to 20s. Keep existing `connect()` and `is_connected()` for other callers if needed.

### 2. Socket leaks in `log.py` on failure
`_post_json()` and `_get_json()` don't wrap socket operations in try/finally. If a flush fails mid-request (DNS timeout, SSL error, etc.), the socket leaks. With the retry pattern, leaked sockets accumulate and can prevent future connections.

**Fix:** Add try/finally to `_post_json()` and `_get_json()` to ensure `s.close()` on any exception.

### 3. NTP failure should be non-fatal
In the test bench there's only one operation (probe). In production we have NTP + flush. NTP failure shouldn't trigger a WiFi disconnect — it's a separate UDP service. Only flush failure (our "probe equivalent") should trigger the disconnect/retry cycle.

```python
            return False
        time.sleep(1)
    print("WiFi connect timed out, status:", self._wlan.status())
    return False
```

```python
    return self._wlan.status() == network.STAT_GOT_IP
```

```python
        if time.time() >= next_sntp_refresh:
            sp.set(NTP, C_PENDING)
            try:
                safe_time.settime(ntptime)
                sp.set(NTP, C_PASS)
            except Exception:
                sp.set(NTP, C_FAIL)
            next_sntp_refresh = time.time() + SNTP_REFRESH_INTERVAL_S

        # Step 3: Flush pending logs (replaces TCP probe as connectivity proof)
        if pending_count() > 0:
            sp.set(LOG, C_PENDING)
            try:
                while flush_once(SHEETS_URL):
                    pass
            except Exception as e:
                print("Flush failed:", e)
                sp.set(LOG, C_FAIL)
                w.disconnect()
                time.sleep(1)
                continue
            sp.set(LOG, C_PASS)

        time.sleep(1)
```

Key changes from current bg_thread:
- **WiFi check uses `has_ip()` + `reconnect()`** instead of `w.connect()` with its retry_interval gating
- **Linear flow with `continue`** on WiFi failure or flush failure (matches test bench)
- **Flush failure triggers `w.disconnect()`** — treats it as connectivity loss, just like test bench's probe failure
- **NTP failure does NOT disconnect** — it's a separate concern
- **Sleep reduced to 1s** (matches test bench's CHECK_INTERVAL) for faster recovery
- **Keeps `while flush_once(): pass`** to drain backlog efficiently when connected

### File 3: `log.py` — Fix socket leaks in `_post_json` and `_get_json`

Wrap socket operations in try/finally to ensure cleanup on exception. This is important because the retry pattern will now trigger more frequent reconnection attempts after failures.

## Files modified
- `wifi.py` — add `reconnect()`, `disconnect()`, `has_ip()`
- `program_tsl.py` — rewrite `bg_thread()` 
- `log.py` — add try/finally to `_post_json()` and `_get_json()`

## Verification
1. Deploy to Pico 2W and observe serial output for the WiFi connect/reconnect cycle
2. Confirm log flushing works when WiFi is stable
3. Test recovery: power-cycle the WiFi AP and verify the bg_thread reconnects and resumes flushing
4. Confirm NTP failure doesn't trigger WiFi disconnect
5. Check that pending logs from before WiFi connection are flushed once connected

        """Return True if WiFi has an IP address (matches test bench's status check)."""
        return self._wlan.status() == network.STAT_GOT_IP

    def disconnect(self):
        """Disconnect from WiFi."""

    def reconnect(self):

        Mirrors the test bench's connect_wifi() pattern: disconnect, reconnect,
        poll status for up to 20s.

                print("WiFi failed, status:", status)
                return False

        print("WiFi connect timed out, status:", self._wlan.status())

        """Return True if WiFi is currently connected."""
        return self._wlan.isconnected()

toolUseResult.originalFile:
"""WiFi connection management for Pico W."""
import network
import time

# Don't mess with this stuff. This sequencing seems to work most of the time, others fail - even
# ones copied diretcly from the Pico W examples sometimes fail to connect reliably.
# And this stuff is basically non-documented. :(

# Do note that sometime the Wifi just will not connect on the first try ever. This code
# does seem to always connect on the 2nd try with my Wifi. 


class WiFi:
    def __init__(self, ssid, password, country=None, retry_interval=30):
        self._ssid = ssid
        self._password = password

        if country:
            print("Setting WiFi country to", country)
            network.country(country)

        # station rather than AP
        self._wlan = network.WLAN(network.STA_IF)

        #load network stack
        self._wlan.active(True)

        # By default the wireless chip will active power-saving mode when it is idle, which might lead it to being less responsive. If
        # you are running a server or need more responsiveness, you can change this by toggling the power mode.
        self._wlan.config(pm = 0xa11140) 

        self._next_retry = time.time()
        self.retry_interval = retry_interval

    def is_error(self):
        """Return True if the WiFi is in a stuck error state (LINK_FAIL or BADAUTH)."""
        return self._wlan.status() in (-1, -3)

    def connect(self):
        """Connect to WiFi, returns True on success.

        The CYW43 radio firmware has an intermittent cold-start bug where
        the first association/authentication attempt fails ~50% of the time
        (EV_SET_SSID returns failure status, or WPA2 handshake fails).
        The delay between active() and connect() is irrelevant — the chip
        is ready, it just sometimes fails the handshake.

        Once in LINK_FAIL (-1) or BADAUTH (-3), the driver is stuck —
        it won't self-recover. We detect this, disconnect (which resets
        wifi_join_state via EV_DISASSOC), and retry within the timeout.
        Second attempt almost always succeeds.
        """

        if (self._wlan.status() <= 0 and time.time() >= self._next_retry):
            print("Failed to connect, status:", self._wlan.status())
            print("Disconnecting due to failed connection attempt...")
            # we seem to need this sometimes to reset the stuck state
            self._wlan.disconnect()
            time.sleep(1)
            print("Re-attempting connection...")
            self._wlan.connect(self._ssid, self._password)
            time.sleep(1)
            self._next_retry = time.time() +  self.retry_interval

        return self._wlan.isconnected()
        

    def is_connected(self):
        """Return True if WiFi is currently connected."""
        return self._wlan.isconnected()

    def mac_hex(self):
        """Return the MAC address as an uppercase hex string."""
        return "".join("%02X" % b for b in self._wlan.config("mac"))

    def ip(self):
        """Return the current IP address string, or None if not connected."""
        if self._wlan.isconnected():
            return self._wlan.ifconfig()[0]
        return None

toolUseResult.originalFile:
"""Append rows to a Google Sheet via Apps Script, with offline resilience.

Foreground: writes each request as a file in pending/, never blocks.
Background: drains pending/ in FIFO order, deleting files on success.

The pending/ directory is the queue. Files are written as .tmp_* (invisible
to background), then atomically renamed to req_* (visible). This guarantees
the background never sees a partially written file. Both os.rename() and
os.remove() are atomic on the Pico's littlefs2 filesystem.
"""
import json
import os
import socket
import ssl
import time
import _thread

PENDING_DIR = "pending"

_seq = 0
_lock = None


def init():
    """Initialize the log module. Call once at startup.

    Creates the pending directory, scans for existing files to avoid
    overwriting on reboot, and allocates a lock for thread safety.
    """
    global _seq, _lock

    try:
        os.mkdir(PENDING_DIR)
    except OSError:
        pass

    try:
        names = [n for n in os.listdir(PENDING_DIR) if n.startswith("req_")]
        if names:
            _seq = max(int(n[4:]) for n in names)
    except OSError:
        pass

    _lock = _thread.allocate_lock()


def log_row(url, data):
    """Queue a row for sending. Returns immediately, never blocks."""
    global _seq
    _seq += 1
    json_str = json.dumps(data)
    tmp_path = "%s/.tmp_%06d" % (PENDING_DIR, _seq)
    req_path = "%s/req_%06d" % (PENDING_DIR, _seq)
    _lock.acquire()
    try:
        with open(tmp_path, "w") as f:
            f.write(json_str)
        os.rename(tmp_path, req_path)
    finally:
        _lock.release()


def pending_count():
    """Return the number of unsent requests in the queue."""
    _lock.acquire()
    try:
        return sum(1 for name in os.listdir(PENDING_DIR) if name.startswith("req_"))
    except OSError:
        return 0
    finally:
        _lock.release()


def _get_json(url):
    """GET a URL over HTTPS, return parsed JSON response."""
    proto, _, host_path = url.split("/", 2)
    host_port, path = host_path.split("/", 1)
    path = "/" + path
    port = 443 if proto == "https:" else 80
    if ":" in host_port:
        host, port = host_port.rsplit(":", 1)
        port = int(port)
    else:
        host = host_port

    hdr = "GET %s HTTP/1.0\r\nHost: %s\r\n\r\n" % (path, host)
    s = socket.socket()
    s.connect(socket.getaddrinfo(host, port)[0][-1])
    if proto == "https:":
        s = ssl.wrap_socket(s, server_hostname=host)
    s.write(hdr.encode())

    # Skip status line and headers
    while s.readline().strip():
        pass
    resp_body = s.read()
    s.close()
    return json.loads(resp_body)


def flush_once(url):
    """Try to send one pending request. Returns True if one was sent."""
    _lock.acquire()
    try:
        names = sorted(n for n in os.listdir(PENDING_DIR) if n.startswith("req_"))
        if not names:
            return False
        name = names[0]
        path = "%s/%s" % (PENDING_DIR, name)
        with open(path, "r") as f:
            data = json.loads(f.read())
    finally:
        _lock.release()

    # Network I/O outside the lock
    result = _post_json(url, data)
    if result.get("message") == "Data appended successfully":
        _lock.acquire()
        try:
            os.remove(path)
        finally:
            _lock.release()
        return True
    else:
        print("Sheet rejected row: %s" % result.get("message", "unknown"))
        raise RuntimeError("Sheet rejected row: %s" % result.get("message", "unknown"))


def _post_json(url, data, max_redirects=3):
    """POST JSON to a URL, following redirects. Returns parsed response JSON.

    MicroPython's requests library converts POST to GET on redirect,
    which breaks Google Apps Script. This uses raw sockets instead.
    """
    body = json.dumps(data).encode()

    for _ in range(max_redirects + 1):
        proto, _, host_path = url.split("/", 2)
        host_port, path = host_path.split("/", 1)
        path = "/" + path
        use_ssl = proto == "https:"
        port = 443 if use_ssl else 80
        if ":" in host_port:
            host, port = host_port.rsplit(":", 1)
            port = int(port)
        else:
            host = host_port

        hdr = "POST %s HTTP/1.0\r\nHost: %s\r\nContent-Type: application/json\r\nContent-Length: %d\r\n\r\n" % (path, host, len(body))

        s = socket.socket()
        s.connect(socket.getaddrinfo(host, port)[0][-1])
        if use_ssl:
            s = ssl.wrap_socket(s, server_hostname=host)
        s.write(hdr.encode() + body)

        # Read status line
        line = s.readline().decode()
        status = int(line.split(" ", 2)[1])

        # Read headers
        location = None
        while True:
            hline = s.readline().decode().strip()
            if not hline:
                break
            if hline.lower().startswith("location:"):
                location = hline.split(":", 1)[1].strip()

        if status in (301, 302, 307, 308) and location:
            # Google Apps Script redirects POST to a GET response URL
            resp_body = s.read()
            s.close()
            return _get_json(location)

        # Read body
        resp_body = s.read()
        s.close()
        return json.loads(resp_body)

    raise RuntimeError("too many redirects")

        while s.readline().strip():
            pass
        resp_body = s.read()
    finally:
        s.close()
    return json.loads(resp_body)


def flush_once(url):
    """Try to send one pending request. Returns True if one was sent."""
    _lock.acquire()
    try:
        names = sorted(n for n in os.listdir(PENDING_DIR) if n.startswith("req_"))
        if not names:
            return False
        name = names[0]
        path = "%s/%s" % (PENDING_DIR, name)
        with open(path, "r") as f:
            data = json.loads(f.read())
    finally:
        _lock.release()

    # Network I/O outside the lock
    result = _post_json(url, data)
    if result.get("message") == "Data appended successfully":
        _lock.acquire()
        try:
            os.remove(path)
        finally:
            _lock.release()
        return True
    else:
        print("Sheet rejected row: %s" % result.get("message", "unknown"))
        raise RuntimeError("Sheet rejected row: %s" % result.get("message", "unknown"))


def _post_json(url, data, max_redirects=3):
    """POST JSON to a URL, following redirects. Returns parsed response JSON.

    MicroPython's requests library converts POST to GET on redirect,
    which breaks Google Apps Script. This uses raw sockets instead.
    """
    body = json.dumps(data).encode()

    for _ in range(max_redirects + 1):
        proto, _, host_path = url.split("/", 2)
        host_port, path = host_path.split("/", 1)
        path = "/" + path
        use_ssl = proto == "https:"
        port = 443 if use_ssl else 80
        if ":" in host_port:
            host, port = host_port.rsplit(":", 1)
            port = int(port)
        else:
            host = host_port

        hdr = "POST %s HTTP/1.0\r\nHost: %s\r\nContent-Type: application/json\r\nContent-Length: %d\r\n\r\n" % (path, host, len(body))

        s = socket.socket()
        s.connect(socket.getaddrinfo(host, port)[0][-1])
        if use_ssl:
            s = ssl.wrap_socket(s, server_hostname=host)
        s.write(hdr.encode() + body)

        # Read status line
        line = s.readline().decode()
        status = int(line.split(" ", 2)[1])

        # Read headers
        location = None
        while True:
            hline = s.readline().decode().strip()
            if not hline:
                break
            if hline.lower().startswith("location:"):
                location = hline.split(":", 1)[1].strip()

        if status in (301, 302, 307, 308) and location:
            # Google Apps Script redirects POST to a GET response URL
            resp_body = s.read()
            s.close()
            return _get_json(location)

        # Read body
        resp_body = s.read()
        s.close()
        return json.loads(resp_body)

    raise RuntimeError("too many redirects")

"""TSL factory programmer with auto-detect and Google Sheets logging.

Autodetects target connection, programs firmware + commissioning timestamp,
verifies, power-cycles the target to run briefly, then logs the result to
a Google Sheet. Waits for target removal before the next cycle.

Requires secrets.py with WIFI_SSID, WIFI_PASSWORD, and SHEETS_URL.

Pin connections below.
"""
import time
import _thread
import safe_time
from sbw import SBW
from secrets import WIFI_SSID, WIFI_PASSWORD, WIFI_COUNTRY, SHEETS_URL, FIRMWARE_URL      
from utils import get_device_uuid
import sbw_native
from pixels import *

# Pico Pin | GPIO | Function
# ---------|------|----------
#       29 | GP22 | SBWTDIO
#       31 | GP26 | SBWTCK (must have ADC)
#       32 | GP27 | VCC
SBW_PIN_CLOCK = 26
SBW_PIN_DATA = 22
SBW_PIN_POWER = 27
POWER_SETTLE_MS = 20
PIXEL_PIN = 28

FIRMWARE_FILE_NAME = "tsl-calibre-msp.txt"

# from part datasheets
DEVICE_DESCRIPTOR_ADDRESS = 0x1A00
DEVICE_DESCRIPTOR_LENGTH = 0x12

# beging of USER FRAM
TIMESTAMP_ADDRESS = 0x1800

# based on part and circuit and firmware
REBOOT_OFF_MS  = 100
# This is dominated by the 0.5s the TSL must wait for the RV3032 to stabilize after power-up.
# This param is tSTART and speced 0.1s typical, 0.5s max. 
BOOT_SETTLE_MS = 700

# emperical acceptable current range for a TSL
LPM_CURRENT_MIN_UA = 1
LPM_CURRENT_MAX_UA = 3

# WiFi timeouts
SNTP_REFRESH_INTERVAL_S = 6000  # refresh SNTP time every 6000 seconds (~100 minutes)

# Pixel indices for headless status strip.
READY   = 0
STATE   = 1

# progress
PROGRAM     = 3
BOOT        = 4
CURRENT     = 5
LOG         = 6

# Startup status steps
CALIBRATE   = 7
WIFI        = 8
NTP         = 9
UPDATE      = 10
SPACER      = 11
# ===============
PIXEL_COUNT = 12

# Semantic pixel colors
C_READY   = GREEN
C_PENDING = LT_YELLOW
C_PASS    = LT_GREEN
C_FAIL    = RED
C_ACTIVE  = YELLOW      # currently active / in-progress


def build_timestamp_bytes(now):
    if isinstance(now, tuple):
        year, month, mday, hour, minute, second, weekday = now[:7]
    else:
        second = now.tm_sec
        minute = now.tm_min
        hour = now.tm_hour
        weekday = now.tm_wday
        mday = now.tm_mday
        month = now.tm_mon
        year = now.tm_year

    return bytes(
        [
            second,
            minute,
            hour,
            weekday,
            mday,
            month,
            year % 100,
        ]
    )


def program_once(power, sbw, firmware_blocks):
    """Program and verify one target. Returns device UUID on success."""
    t0 = time.ticks_ms()

    def tp(msg):
        """Print with ##.### seconds timestamp."""
        dt = time.ticks_diff(time.ticks_ms(), t0)
        print("%02d.%03d %s" % (dt // 1000, dt % 1000, msg))

    now = safe_time.gmtime()

    tp("Reading JTAG ID...")
    ok, jtag_id = sbw.read_id()
    if not ok or jtag_id != sbw_native.JTAG_ID_EXPECTED:
        raise RuntimeError("expected JTAG ID 0x%02X, found 0x%02X" % (sbw_native.JTAG_ID_EXPECTED, jtag_id))

    tp("Reading device descriptor...")
    ok, dd_bytes = sbw.read_bytes(DEVICE_DESCRIPTOR_ADDRESS, DEVICE_DESCRIPTOR_LENGTH)
    if not ok or len(dd_bytes) != DEVICE_DESCRIPTOR_LENGTH:
        raise RuntimeError("failed to read device descriptor")

    device_uuid = get_device_uuid(dd_bytes)
    tp("Device UUID is %s" % device_uuid)

    # Write timestamp + firmware
    timestamp_data = build_timestamp_bytes(now)
    tp("Writing commissioning timestamp...")
    if not sbw.write_bytes(TIMESTAMP_ADDRESS, timestamp_data):
        raise RuntimeError("write failed for timestamp at 0x%05X" % TIMESTAMP_ADDRESS)

    for index, (address, data) in enumerate(firmware_blocks, start=1):
        tp("Writing firmware block %d/%d addr=0x%05X len=%d..." % (
            index, len(firmware_blocks), address & 0xFFFFF, len(data)))
        if not sbw.write_bytes(address, data):
            raise RuntimeError("write failed at 0x%05X" % (address & 0xFFFFF))

    # Verify all
    tp("Verifying commissioning timestamp...")
    ok, _ = sbw.verify_bytes(TIMESTAMP_ADDRESS, timestamp_data)
    if not ok:
        raise RuntimeError("verify failed for timestamp at 0x%05X" % TIMESTAMP_ADDRESS)

    for index, (address, data) in enumerate(firmware_blocks, start=1):
        tp("Verifying firmware block %d/%d addr=0x%05X len=%d..." % (
            index, len(firmware_blocks), address & 0xFFFFF, len(data)))
        ok, _ = sbw.verify_bytes(address, data)
        if not ok:
            raise RuntimeError("verify failed at 0x%05X" % (address & 0xFFFFF))

    tp("All blocks verified.")

    return device_uuid


import log
from log import log_row, flush_once, pending_count

# Do wifi connection and logging in a background thread so it doesn't block programming
# we share both the pixel display and the log folder with the main thread, so we added semaphore locks
# for those (sp and the log folder)

def bg_thread(sp,w):

    print("Background thread started.")

    import ntptime
    import safe_time

    # did we start up with a backlog?
    if pending_count() > 0:
        print("Backlog detected: %d pending rows" % pending_count())
        sp.set(LOG, C_PENDING)
    else:
        sp.set(LOG, C_PASS)

    next_sntp_refresh = time.time() 

    while True:

        if w.connect():
            sp.set(WIFI, C_PASS)

            if (time.time() >= next_sntp_refresh):
                sp.set(NTP, C_PENDING)
                try:
                    safe_time.settime(ntptime)
                    sp.set(NTP, C_PASS)
                except Exception:
                    sp.set(NTP, C_FAIL)
                next_sntp_refresh = time.time() + SNTP_REFRESH_INTERVAL_S       


            if (pending_count() > 0):
                sp.set(LOG, C_PENDING)
                try:
                    while flush_once(SHEETS_URL):
                        pass
                    sp.set(LOG, C_PASS)
                except Exception as e:
                    sp.set(LOG, C_FAIL)
                    print("Failed to flush log: %s" % e)

        else:
            if w.is_error():
                sp.set(WIFI, C_FAIL)
                print("WiFi error detected, status:", w._wlan.status())
            else:
                sp.set(WIFI, C_PENDING)

        time.sleep(5)

def program_loop():

    print("Testing status pixels...")
    sp = StatusPixels(PIXEL_PIN, PIXEL_COUNT)
    for color in [RED, GREEN, BLUE,BLACK]:
        for i in range(PIXEL_COUNT):
            sp.set(i, color)
            time.sleep_ms(100)
        time.sleep_ms(500)

    print("Initializing modules...")
    import machine
    import network
    from target_power import TargetPower, TargetPowerWithDetect
    from utils import load_firmware_blocks, firmware_hash

    # Start Wifi and get MAC before starting bg thread — both threads accessing WLAN can deadlock
    from wifi import WiFi
    w = WiFi(WIFI_SSID, WIFI_PASSWORD, WIFI_COUNTRY)
    mac_addr = w.mac_hex()

    # load firmware touches the file system, so load it before starting the background thread
    print("Loading firmware file %s into memory..." % FIRMWARE_FILE_NAME)
    firmware_blocks = load_firmware_blocks(FIRMWARE_FILE_NAME)
    fw_hash = firmware_hash(firmware_blocks)
    print(
        "Firmware is %d block(s), %d bytes.\n"
        % (len(firmware_blocks), sum(len(data) for _, data in firmware_blocks))
    )

    # get log ready for both threads
    log.init()

    # Read calibration file before bg starts file access
    try:
        r_pullup = TargetPower.load_calibration()
        print("Pull-up calibration: %.0f ohm" % r_pullup)
        sp.set(CALIBRATE, C_PASS)
    except Exception as e:
        print("Failed to load pull-up calibration: %s" % e)
        sp.set(CALIBRATE, C_FAIL)
        sp.set(READY, C_FAIL)   
        print("HALTED. Run current_measure_calibrate to fix.")
        while True:
            time.sleep_ms(1000)


    print("Starting background thread...")
    _thread.start_new_thread(bg_thread, (sp,w))

    power = TargetPowerWithDetect(SBW_PIN_POWER, SBW_PIN_CLOCK, POWER_SETTLE_MS)
    sbw = SBW(SBW_PIN_CLOCK, SBW_PIN_DATA)
    led = machine.Pin("LED", machine.Pin.OUT, value=0)


    while True:
        print("Waiting for target...")
        sp.set(READY, C_READY)
        power.wait_for_connect()

        print("Target detected.")
        sp.set(READY, BLACK)
        sp.set(PROGRAM, C_PENDING)

        device_uuid = None
        status = "fail"
        current_ua = None

        print("Powering on target...")
        power.on()

        led.on()
        try:
            device_uuid = program_once(power, sbw, firmware_blocks)
            status = "programmed"
            sp.set(PROGRAM, C_PASS)
        except Exception as exc:
            print("Programming failed: %s" % exc)
            sp.set(PROGRAM, C_FAIL)
            sp.set(STATE, C_FAIL)
        finally:
            led.off()

        # no need to check current if programming failed
        if status == "programmed":
            # Reboot target so newly programmed firmware starts running
            print("Rebooting target...")
            sp.set(BOOT, C_PENDING)
            power.off()
            time.sleep_ms(REBOOT_OFF_MS)
            time.sleep_ms(5000)
            power.on()
            # Wait for the RV3032 to stabilize after power-up before attempting to communicate with it
            print("Waiting %dms for target to start up..." % BOOT_SETTLE_MS)
            time.sleep_ms(BOOT_SETTLE_MS)
            sp.set(BOOT, C_PASS)

            # By the time we get to here, the TSL should be at the "TEST ONLY" screen where power usage is representative. 
            # Measure LPM current to test for defects
            sp.set(CURRENT, C_PENDING)
            print("Switching target power to internal pull-up for current measurement...")
            adc = power.power_via_pullup()
            time.sleep_ms(1000)
            current_ua = power.measure_current_ua(adc, r_pullup, count=10, period_ms=10)
            time.sleep_ms(1000)
            if current_ua is None:
                status = "brownout"
                print("Brownout — target not in LPM.")
                sp.set(CURRENT, C_FAIL)
                sp.set(STATE, C_FAIL)
            elif current_ua < LPM_CURRENT_MIN_UA or current_ua > LPM_CURRENT_MAX_UA:
                status = "lpm_fail"
                print("Target current out of LPM spec: %.0f µA" % current_ua)
                sp.set(CURRENT, C_FAIL)
                sp.set(STATE, C_FAIL)
            else:
                status = "pass"
                print("Target current draw: %.0f µA" % current_ua)
                sp.set(CURRENT, C_PASS)
                sp.set(STATE, C_PASS)

        # Power off target
        print("Powering off target and releasing SBW...")
        sbw.release()
        power.off()

        # Queue log row
        now = safe_time.gmtime()
        timestamp = "%04d-%02d-%02d %02d:%02d:%02d" % (now[0], now[1], now[2], now[3], now[4], now[5])
        log_row(SHEETS_URL, [device_uuid or "unknown", timestamp, fw_hash, mac_addr, status, current_ua])

        sp.set(LOG,C_PENDING)

        print("Waiting for target to be removed...")
        power.wait_for_disconnect()
        print("Target removed.\n")

        #clear progress from previous run
        sp.set(PROGRAM, BLACK)
        sp.set(BOOT, BLACK)
        sp.set(CURRENT, BLACK)
        sp.set(LOG, BLACK)
        sp.set(STATE, BLACK)

for i in range(10):
    print(f"Starting in {10 - i} seconds...")
    time.sleep(1)
    
program_loop()

content.text:
All three files updated. Summary of changes:

**`wifi.py`** — Added `has_ip()`, `disconnect()`, and `reconnect()` methods. `reconnect()` mirrors the test bench's `connect_wifi()`: full disconnect, reconnect, poll status for up to 20s.

**`program_tsl.py`** — Rewrote `bg_thread` to match the test bench's linear flow:
- `has_ip()` check → `reconnect()` if no IP → `continue` on failure
- NTP sync (non-fatal, no disconnect on failure)
- Log flush → on exception: `disconnect()` + `continue` (treats flush failure like the test bench treats probe failure)
- Sleep reduced from 5s to 1s

**`log.py`** — Added try/finally to `_post_json()` and `_get_json()` so sockets are always closed on failure, preventing leaks during the retry cycle.

message.content: it looks like program_tsl is using `if not w.has_ip()` and the test code uses `  if wlan.status() != network.STAT_GOT_IP`. Any reason why these are different?

content.text:
No good reason — `has_ip()` wraps exactly that check, so they're functionally identical. I just encapsulated it in a method to avoid reaching into `w._wlan` directly from `bg_thread`. The WiFi class owns the `wlan` object, so going through a method felt cleaner than `w._wlan.status() != network.STAT_GOT_IP`.

If you'd prefer `bg_thread` to use the raw check directly (closer to the test bench), I can do that instead.

file.content:
"""WiFi connection management for Pico W."""
import network
import time

# Don't mess with this stuff. This sequencing seems to work most of the time, others fail - even
# ones copied diretcly from the Pico W examples sometimes fail to connect reliably.
# And this stuff is basically non-documented. :(

# Do note that sometime the Wifi just will not connect on the first try ever. This code
# does seem to always connect on the 2nd try with my Wifi. 


class WiFi:
    def __init__(self, ssid, password, country=None, retry_interval=30):
        self._ssid = ssid
        self._password = password

        if country:
            print("Setting WiFi country to", country)
            network.country(country)

        # station rather than AP
        self._wlan = network.WLAN(network.STA_IF)

        #load network stack
        self._wlan.active(True)

        # By default the wireless chip will active power-saving mode when it is idle, which might lead it to being less responsive. If
        # you are running a server or need more responsiveness, you can change this by toggling the power mode.
        self._wlan.config(pm = 0xa11140) 

        self._next_retry = time.time()
        self.retry_interval = retry_interval

    def is_error(self):
        """Return True if the WiFi is in a stuck error state (LINK_FAIL or BADAUTH)."""
        return self._wlan.status() in (-1, -3)

    def connect(self):
        """Connect to WiFi, returns True on success.

        The CYW43 radio firmware has an intermittent cold-start bug where
        the first association/authentication attempt fails ~50% of the time
        (EV_SET_SSID returns failure status, or WPA2 handshake fails).
        The delay between active() and connect() is irrelevant — the chip
        is ready, it just sometimes fails the handshake.

        Once in LINK_FAIL (-1) or BADAUTH (-3), the driver is stuck —
        it won't self-recover. We detect this, disconnect (which resets
        wifi_join_state via EV_DISASSOC), and retry within the timeout.
        Second attempt almost always succeeds.
        """

        if (self._wlan.status() <= 0 and time.time() >= self._next_retry):
            print("Failed to connect, status:", self._wlan.status())
            print("Disconnecting due to failed connection attempt...")
            # we seem to need this sometimes to reset the stuck state
            self._wlan.disconnect()
            time.sleep(1)
            print("Re-attempting connection...")
            self._wlan.connect(self._ssid, self._password)
            time.sleep(1)
            self._next_retry = time.time() +  self.retry_interval

        return self._wlan.isconnected()
        

    def has_ip(self):
        """Return True if WiFi has an IP address (matches test bench's status check)."""
        return self._wlan.status() == network.STAT_GOT_IP

    def disconnect(self):
        """Disconnect from WiFi."""
        self._wlan.disconnect()

    def reconnect(self):
        """Full disconnect/reconnect cycle with polling. Returns True if connected.

        Mirrors the test bench's connect_wifi() pattern: disconnect, reconnect,
        poll status for up to 20s.
        """
        self._wlan.disconnect()
        time.sleep(1)
        print("Connecting to WiFi:", self._ssid)
        self._wlan.connect(self._ssid, self._password)
        for _ in range(20):
            status = self._wlan.status()
            if status == network.STAT_GOT_IP:
                print("WiFi connected:", self._wlan.ipconfig("addr4"))
                return True
            if status < 0:
                print("WiFi failed, status:", status)
                return False
            time.sleep(1)
        print("WiFi connect timed out, status:", self._wlan.status())
        return False

    def is_connected(self):
        """Return True if WiFi is currently connected."""
        return self._wlan.isconnected()

    def mac_hex(self):
        """Return the MAC address as an uppercase hex string."""
        return "".join("%02X" % b for b in self._wlan.config("mac"))

    def ip(self):
        """Return the current IP address string, or None if not connected."""
        if self._wlan.isconnected():
            return self._wlan.ifconfig()[0]
        return None
